title: Lecturer Quiz: PyTorch & Hyperparameter Tuning - Multiple Choice

questions.question: What is the purpose of hyperparameter tuning in the context of machine learning?

options.A: To find the optimal configuration for hyperparameters
options.B: To increase the complexity of the neural network
options.C: To adjust the training data to better fit the model
options.D: To automatically generate new features for improved accuracy

questions.question: In PyTorch, what is the primary function of the nn.Linear module?

options.A: It defines the forward pass of the model
options.B: It applies an activation function to the layer's output
options.C: It makes the layer's input data linealry independent
options.D: It connects each input in a layer to every output using a linear transformation

questions.question: Why is nn.ModuleList commonly used in PyTorch for defining neural network architectures?

options.A: It allows for a dynamic creation of layers by registering all modules it contains as part of a model
options.B: It automatically computes gradients during backpropagation, making it necessary for training
options.C: It stores the values of the activations in the forward pass as a list to be backpropagated through when updating the parameters
options.D: It enforces sequential execution of layers

questions.question: In PyTorch, when using the CrossEntropyLoss for classification, we don't add an activation function after the output layer. Why?

options.A: The CrossEntropyLoss works with the raw, unnormalized scores (logits)
options.B: The CrossEntropyLoss assumes the use of the sigmoid activation function
options.C: This is a bad practice; Not adding an activation function after the output layer may cause numerical instability in this case
options.D: The CrossEntropyLoss is incompatible with any activation function

questions.question: When training an MLP in PyTorch, what is the purpose of the torch.optim module?

options.A: It optimizes the model parameters using gradient descent.
options.B: It specifies the number of hidden layers in the model.
options.C: It defines the loss function for the model.
options.D: It computes the gradients during backpropagation.

questions.question:
Select the correct alternatives based on this code: def train_model(MLP_model, num_epochs = 10): MLP_model.to(device) # Move model to device # Define the loss function and optimizer criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss() optimizer = optim.SGD(MLP_model.parameters(), lr=0.01) # Training loop for epoch in range(num_epochs): total_loss = 0 for inputs, labels in train_loader: inputs, labels = inputs.to(device), labels.to(device) # Move data to device optimizer.zero_grad() # Zero the gradients inputs = inputs.view(-1, 28 * 28) # Flatten the images outputs = MLP_model(inputs) # Forward pass loss = criterion(outputs, labels) # Compute the loss loss.backward() # Backward pass optimizer.step() # Update the weights total_loss += loss.item() print(f'Epoch [{epoch + 1}/{num_epochs}], Loss: {total_loss / len(train_loader)}') 1) In the line: MLP_model.to(device) what is the purpose of calling .to(device) on the model? [ Select ] 2) In the line: inputs = inputs.view(-1, 28 * 28) why is the input reshaped this way? [ Select ]

Select the correct choice for blank #1:

options.A: It freezes all parameters so they no longer update during training
options.B: It moves all model parameters and buffers to the CPU or GPU specified by device, ensuring computations happen on the correct hardware
options.C: It automatically tunes the learning rate to match the device speed
options.D: It converts the model into a TorchScript module for faster inference

questions.question:
Select the correct alternatives based on this code: def train_model(MLP_model, num_epochs = 10): MLP_model.to(device) # Move model to device # Define the loss function and optimizer criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss() optimizer = optim.SGD(MLP_model.parameters(), lr=0.01) # Training loop for epoch in range(num_epochs): total_loss = 0 for inputs, labels in train_loader: inputs, labels = inputs.to(device), labels.to(device) # Move data to device optimizer.zero_grad() # Zero the gradients inputs = inputs.view(-1, 28 * 28) # Flatten the images outputs = MLP_model(inputs) # Forward pass loss = criterion(outputs, labels) # Compute the loss loss.backward() # Backward pass optimizer.step() # Update the weights total_loss += loss.item() print(f'Epoch [{epoch + 1}/{num_epochs}], Loss: {total_loss / len(train_loader)}') 1) In the line: MLP_model.to(device) what is the purpose of calling .to(device) on the model? [ Select ] 2) In the line: inputs = inputs.view(-1, 28 * 28) why is the input reshaped this way? [ Select ]

Select the correct choice for blank #2:

options.A: Because SGD can only operate on flattened tensors
options.B: Because the MLP expects 1-dimensional feature vectors, so each 28×28 image must be flattened into a single vector before being fed into the first fully connected layer
options.D: Because flattening reduces overfitting by removing spatial correlations
